title: Quiz: Linear Optical Gates
description: Test your understanding of beam splitters, phase shifters, and the KLM scheme

questions.question: What happens when two indistinguishable photons enter a 50:50 beam splitter from different input ports (Hong-Ou-Mandel effect)?

options.text: Each photon randomly exits from one of the two output ports
Both photons always exit from the same output port
The photons are absorbed by the beam splitter
One photon is transmitted and one is reflected

questions.explanation: In the Hong-Ou-Mandel effect, two indistinguishable photons entering a 50:50 beam splitter from different ports always exit together from the same port. The input state |1,1> transforms to (|2,0> + |0,2>)/sqrt(2). This quantum interference has no classical analog.

questions.question: Why can't a standard CNOT gate be directly implemented with linear optics?

options.text: Linear optics can only operate on single photons
Photons travel too fast for gate operations
Photons do not naturally interact with each other
Beam splitters are not precise enough

questions.explanation: Photons do not naturally interact with each other - they pass through one another without effect. Two-qubit gates require an effective interaction between qubits, which must be induced through measurement in the KLM scheme.

questions.question: What is measurement-induced nonlinearity in the KLM scheme?

options.text: Using nonlinear crystals to create photon interactions
Conditioning on specific measurement outcomes to achieve effective nonlinear operations
Measuring photons at very high intensities
Using feedback to adjust beam splitter reflectivities

questions.explanation: Measurement-induced nonlinearity uses post-selected measurements to project the system into desired states. By conditioning on specific measurement outcomes (and discarding others), effective nonlinear operations on photons can be achieved.

questions.question: For polarization-encoded qubits, which optical element implements a Hadamard-like operation?
questions.explanation: A half-wave plate (HWP) at 22.5 degrees (pi/8 radians) from the horizontal implements a Hadamard gate for polarization qubits: |H> -> (|H> + |V>)/sqrt(2) and |V> -> (|H> - |V>)/sqrt(2).

questions.question: How many beam splitters are needed in the Reck decomposition of an n-mode unitary?
questions.explanation: The Reck decomposition uses n(n-1)/2 beam splitters (or MZIs) arranged in a triangular array. For example, an 8-mode unitary requires 28 beam splitters.

questions.question: The success probability of KLM-style two-qubit gates can be made arbitrarily close to 1 using gate teleportation with polynomial resource overhead.
questions.explanation: True! KLM showed that by preparing entangled resource states offline and using gate teleportation, the success probability can be boosted to 1 - epsilon with resources scaling as O(log^c(1/epsilon)), which is polynomial in the desired precision.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of the Clements decomposition over the Reck decomposition?
questions.explanation: The Clements decomposition achieves depth n compared to depth 2n-3 for Reck. This is important because lower depth means fewer sequential operations and therefore less accumulated loss in physical implementations.

questions.question: For a phase shifter applying phase phi, what transformation does it perform on a Fock state |n>?
questions.explanation: A phase shifter U_PS(phi) = e^(i*phi*n_hat) acting on Fock state |n> gives e^(i*n*phi)|n>. The phase acquired is proportional to the photon number n, which is why this creates a nonlinear phase for multi-photon states.
